coredata.dc:title: Characterization and enhanced nonlinear optical limiting response in carbon nanodots dispersed in solid-state hybrid organically modified silica gel glasses
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Li
Zheng, Chan
Guo, Qiaohang
Huang, Dongdong
Wu, Xiukai
Chen, Ling
coredata.dc:description: Freely dispersed carbon nanodots (CNDs) were introduced into a 3-glycidoxy-propyltrimethoxysilane modified silicate gel glass (i.e. an organically modified silica or ORMOSIL) by a highly efficient and simple sol-gel process, which could be easily extended to prepare functional molecules/nanoparticles solid state optoelectronic devices. Scanning electron microscope imaging, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, pore structure measurements, ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to investigate the surface characteristics, structure, texture, and linear optical properties of the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses. Images and UV/Vis spectra confirmed the successful dispersion of CNDs in the ORMOSIL gel glass. The surface characteristics and pore structure of the host SiO2 matrix were markedly changed through the introduction of the CNDs. The linear optical properties of the guest CNDs were also affected by the sol-gel procedure. The nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of the CNDs were investigated by a nanosecond open-aperture Z-scan technique at 532 nm both in liquid and solid matrices. We found that the NLO response of the CNDs was considerably improved after their incorporation into the ORMOSIL gel glasses. Possible enhancement mechanisms were also explored. The nonlinear extinction coefficient gradually increased while the optical limiting (OL) threshold decreased as the CND doping level was increased. This result suggests that the NLO and OL properties of the composite gel glasses can be optimized by tuning the concentration of CNDs in the gel glass matrix. Our findings show that CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses are promising candidates for optical limiters to protect sensitive instruments and human eyes from damage caused by high power lasers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sol-gel processes
Carbon nanodots
Nanocomposites
Optical limiting properties
Silicate

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of as-prepared CNDs.
Photographs of CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses (doping levels of 5.5, 7.4, 11.1, and 14.8 × 10−4 from left to right).
SEM images of silica gel glass (a) and CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass (b) (doping level was 14.8 × 10−4).
FT-IR spectra of the (A) CND powder, (B) SiO2, and (C) SiO2/CND ORMOSIL gel glass.
Typical N2 gas adsorption–desorption isotherms (a) and the corresponding pore size distributions (b) of CND/SiO2 gel-glasses with different doping levels.

(a) UV–vis spectra, (b) diagram used to calculate the band gap of the CND aqueous solution and CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses with different doping level, and (c) dependence of band gap on CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses doping level.
Fluorescence spectra of (a) CND aqueous solution, (b) SiO2 gel glass, and (c) CND/SiO2 gel-glasses (doping level of 11.1 × 10−4). All spectral emission intensities are normalized.

OA Z-scan curves of CND aqueous solution, CND/SiO2 gel-glasses (doping levels of 11.1 × 10−4), and SiO2 matrix at an input intensity of 15.28 μJ (a) and CND/SiO2 gel-glasses (doping levels of 11.1 × 10−4) and GO gel glass with the same linear transmittance of 80% at input intensity of 15.37 μJ (b).
OA Z-scan curves (a) and OL curves (b) of CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses with different doping level at input intensity of 15.66 μJ.
Dependence of nonlinear absorption coefficient (β) on CND/SiO2 gel glasses doping level.
OA Z-scan curves of CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses with different repetition frequency.
Synthesis of transparent ORMOSIL gel glasses incorporating well-dispersed CNDs.

head.ce:title: Characterization and enhanced nonlinear optical limiting response in carbon nanodots dispersed in solid-state hybrid organically modified silica gel glasses

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Fujian University of Technology, 3 Xueyuan Road, Fuzhou 350108, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Freely dispersed carbon nanodots (CNDs) were introduced into a 3-glycidoxy-propyltrimethoxysilane modified silicate gel glass (i.e. an organically modified silica or ORMOSIL) by a highly efficient and simple sol-gel process, which could be easily extended to prepare functional molecules/nanoparticles solid state optoelectronic devices. Scanning electron microscope imaging, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, pore structure measurements, ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to investigate the surface characteristics, structure, texture, and linear optical properties of the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses. Images and UV/Vis spectra confirmed the successful dispersion of CNDs in the ORMOSIL gel glass. The surface characteristics and pore structure of the host SiO2 matrix were markedly changed through the introduction of the CNDs. The linear optical properties of the guest CNDs were also affected by the sol-gel procedure. The nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of the CNDs were investigated by a nanosecond open-aperture Z-scan technique at 532 nm both in liquid and solid matrices. We found that the NLO response of the CNDs was considerably improved after their incorporation into the ORMOSIL gel glasses. Possible enhancement mechanisms were also explored. The nonlinear extinction coefficient gradually increased while the optical limiting (OL) threshold decreased as the CND doping level was increased. This result suggests that the NLO and OL properties of the composite gel glasses can be optimized by tuning the concentration of CNDs in the gel glass matrix. Our findings show that CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses are promising candidates for optical limiters to protect sensitive instruments and human eyes from damage caused by high power lasers.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the advent of the laser in 1960, this technology has been widely used in many fields including medicine, information technology, accurate measuring, chemistry, material processing, material preparation, and in military applications [1–3]. Consequently, interest has also been aroused for the development optical limiting (OL) materials, which feature a decreased transmittance for high intensity laser inputs. Such materials can be used to protect eyes and sensitive instruments from laser induced damage. Over the past decade, considerable efforts have been devoted to the development of ideal broadband OL materials based on mechanisms such as nonlinear scattering (NLS) and nonlinear absorption (NLA) [4–12]. Strong OL activity has been observed in a wide range of materials, including organic dye molecules (e.g., porphyrins [4] and phthalocyanines [5]), high molecular polymers (e.g., polythiophenes [6] and polyanilines [7]), metallic [8] and semiconducting nanoparticles [9], and carbon nanomaterials [10–12]. Among these materials systems, carbon-based nanomaterials, including fullerenes, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, and carbon black, show excellent potential for use as OL materials and have been widely applied in the field of nonlinear optics. For example, CNTs exhibit unique broadband OL properties from visible to near infrared wavelengths, in the nanosecond regime, owing to NLS from scattering centers consisting of solvent bubbles and ionized carbon microplasma.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have emerged as a particularly interesting carbon nanomaterial, which has drawn considerable attention owing to its unique properties, including: excellent chemical and photostability, biocompatibility, easy functionalization, and up-conversion of photoluminescence. These features have been applied in fields such as bioimaging, solar light harvesting, chemical and biochemical analysis, photocatalysis, and optoelectronics [13–15]. Notably, CNDs have recently been shown to exhibit excellent nonlinear optical (NLO) and OL responses [16–19]. Liang et al. found that the third-order nonlinear susceptibility χ(3) of CNDs is strongly dependent on the diameter of the CNDs and the carboxyl groups on the surface of the CNDs [16]. Li et al. investigated the third-order nonlinear optical response of CNDs with single 190-fs laser pulses and revealed that nonlinear reflection and absorption were associated with the concentration of CNDs [17]. Dezhi et al. observed strong OL effects in CNDs with an energy threshold of 74 mJ/cm2 for 800-nm femtosecond laser pulses; the OL effects of CNDs were mainly attributed to two-photon absorption [18]. All these reports confirmed that CNDs are a potential candidate OL material, which can be applied in nonlinear optics. However, many of the above studies were performed in solvent. Although liquid matrices are convenient for investigations of material properties and mechanisms of phenomena, solutions measurements are not generally representative of the practical environment in devices and other applications. Rather, solid-state measurements are of greater utility for preparing device components and for studying underlying optoelectronic mechanisms. Therefore, the incorporation of the CNDs into solid matrices is important for practical applications of the materials.

In the present study, we report a highly efficient and convenient sol-gel synthesis of transparent ORMOSIL gel glasses incorporating well-dispersed CNDs (as shown in Scheme 1 ). This approach could be easily extended to prepare functional molecule/nanoparticle solid state optoelectronic devices. The additive 3-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane [CH2OCHCH2O(CH2)3Si(OCH3)3, GPTMS] helped to improve the elasticity and flexibility of the resultant gel glass and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane [NH2(CH2)3Si(OC2H5)3, APTES] acted as a weak organic basic catalyst to promote polycondensation. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) imaging, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, pore structure measurements, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy were performed to investigate the morphology, structure, texture, and linear optical properties of the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses. The NLO properties of the CNDs in water and OMOSIL gel glass were investigated with the use of a nanosecond open-aperture (OA) Z-scan technique at 532 nm. The results were compared and possible mechanisms for the enhancement of OL behavior of the CNDs in the ORMOSIL gel glass were explored.

CNDs were prepared by a hydrothermal synthesis using heparin sodium as a raw material. A 40-mL portion of 0.0275 g/mL sodium heparin solution was added to a 50-mL stainless-steel autoclave and reacted in an oven for 12 h at 140 °C. The reacted black mixture was filtered and the filtrate was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min at 23 °C. After vacuum filtration (filter membrane: 0.22 μm) and dialysis (MWCO = 3500/8000) a light brown supernatant was obtained. The dialyzed solution was then dried in a drying air oven for 48 h (60 °C) to obtain a concentrated CND solution. The solution was further dried to obtain the CND powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses
ce:section.ce:para: Optically transparent CND/SiO2 OMOSIL gel glasses were fabricated through hydrolysis and polycondensation of tetraethyloxysilane (TEOS), GPTMS, and APTES. The doping levels of CNDs were 5.5 × 10−4, 7.4 × 10−4, 11.1 × 10−4, and 14.8 × 10−4 (mass ratio of CND powder to SiO2). The molar ratio of (TEOS+GPTMS+APTES), ethanol, and distilled water in the precursor was 1:4:4; the molar ratio of TEOS, GPTMS, and APTES was 7:2:1. In addition, N,N′-dimethyl formamide (DMF) was introduced as a solvent and drying control chemical additive at a proportion of 0.6 DMF per ethanol volume ratio. Specifically, 10, 2.65, 1.4, 15, and 4.5 mL of TEOS, GPTMS, APTES, ethanol, and water were respectively mixed by ultrasonication for 30 min. Subsequently, 5 mL of a DMF suspension containing different contents of CNDs was gradually added to the mixture, which was then ultrasonicated for 4 h. The resulting mixture was divided into several parts of equal volume, each of which was individually cast in a polystyrene cell, sealed, and left to age and dry for several weeks. All the gel glasses were approximately 1.3 mm thick and 2.5 cm in diameter.

For comparison, graphene oxide (GO) doped silica (GO/SiO2) gel glass was also prepared following a similar synthetic procedure to that for the CND/SiO2 gel glass except that 5 mL of a DMF suspension containing GO was introduced.

The geometry of the obtained CNDs was investigated with a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEM-2010; accelerating voltage, 200 kV). For the TEM observations, the samples were ultrasonicated in ethanol to ensure even dispersion. A drop of the dispersed sample was left to dry on a commercial, carbon-coated Cu TEM grid. The morphology of the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass was examined with a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE SEM: JEOL JSM- 6700, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). For the SEM observations, Au was deposited onto a freshly fractured surface of the sample by sputtering. FT-IR spectra were measured on a ThermoNICOLET6700 spectrometer. The samples were prepared as KBr pellets. The pore structure of the CNDs/SiO2 ORMOSIL was determined from nitrogen absorption isotherms measured at 77 K with the use of a Micromeritics 3Flex Surface Area and Porosimetry Analyzer. The samples were degassed at 120 °C for 10 h prior to the measurements. UV–vis spectra were measured on a Cary 50 spectrophotometer (UV-2600). The gel glasses were fixed vertically in a quartz cell using plasticine. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed on a Horbia Jobin Yvon FM-4 fluorescence spectrometer. The hardness of the gel glass was measured with a Hysitron (TI-Premier) nanoindizer.

The NLO behavior of CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses was examined by the OA Z-scan technique [19]. The excitation light source was an Nd:YAG laser (Brio 640, Quantel, Les Ulis, France) with a repetition rate of 1 Hz. The laser pulses (period, 4 ns; wavelength, 532 nm) were split into two beams by a beam splitter and one beam was focused with a lens with a focal length of 30 cm. The pulse energies at the front and back of the sample were monitored with energy detectors D1 and D2 (PE25, Ophir Optronics Solutions Ltd., Jerusalem, Israel). The laser beam spot diameter was approximately 14.5 μm. All measurements were conducted at room temperature. Sample solution was placed in a 1 mm-thick glass cuvette. The hybrid gel glasses were fixed vertically with a clamp. Each sample was mounted onto a computer-controlled translation stage that shifted the sample along the z-axis.

The morphology of the CNDs was elucidated from TEM images. Fig. 1 (a) shows a representative TEM image of the CNDs and the inset shows a corresponding size distribution histogram. The TEM image indicates that the CNDs were well-dispersed and uniform. Furthermore, the nanodots were clearly monodisperse spherical particles with diameters ranging between 3 and 5 nm, as indicated by size histogram in Fig. 1 (a), obtained from measurements of 100 randomly selected particles. To investigate the crystal lattice fringes and particle sizes of the obtained CNDs, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained from a typical region of the nanostructure, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The image confirmed that the as-prepared CNDs exhibited a relatively uniform and well-dispersed spherical shape. The spacings of the crystal lattice were 0.204 and 0.2 nm, corresponding to the (322) and (410) planes, respectively, confirming the high crystallinity of the as-synthesized CNDs.

Fig. 2 shows a digital photograph of the CND-doped silica gel glass with doping levels of 5.5, 7.4, 11.1, and 14.8 × 10−4, respectively. The lowest doping level gel glass appeared to be transparent to the naked eye and became yellow in appearance as the doping level increased. All the gel glasses were approximately 1.5 mm in thickness and 25 mm in diameter. No visible sediment could be detected by visual inspection indicating that the CNDs in the hybrid glass were homogeneously dispersed and solidified. The hybrid glasses were also completely free of cracks, reflecting mechanical properties suitable for future practical applications.

Fig. 3 shows a fracture surface of the silica gel glass and the CNDs/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass, at a doping level of 14.8 × 10−4. The CNDs could not be detected in the images because of the low doping level in the gel glasses. In Fig. 3, both samples were composed of round nanoparticles or aggregates. The granules of the silica gel glasses were small and homogeneous, and the surface appeared to have a finer texture than that of the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass. The granules of the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass were larger and its surface texture was coarser. This result can be attributed to the presence of the CNDs in the precursor, which might influence the hydrolysis and polycondensation of TEOS and cause silica granules to grow around them through inhomogeneous nucleation.

FT-IR spectroscopy is a powerful tool used for investigating structural changes in sol-gel-derived glasses. Fig. 4 shows FT-IR spectra of the CND powder, the silica gel glass and CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass. In the CND powder, broad bands at 3414 and 1667 cm−1 were attributed to -NH2 and -C=O stretching respectively. Sharp absorptions arising from the -C-O-C- of aromatic ester appeared at 1139 cm−1. In addition, -CH2 bending was found at 1396 cm−1. Peaks at 624 and 519 cm−1 in the fingerprint region corresponded to -C-S and -OH bond vibrations, respectively [20,21]. The large amount of hydrophilic surface groups, including –OH and –NH2, made the CNDs hydrophilic, which contributed to the stability of their aqueous dispersions and ensured their homogenous dispersion in the SiO2 gel glass. For the SiO2 gel glass, four fundamental absorption bands were observed at 1066, 795, 660, and 460 cm−1, originating from the anti-symmetric stretching vibration of Si-O-Si, symmetric stretching vibration of the Si-O-Si, ring vibrations of [SiO4] tetrahedral units, and asymmetrical bending vibration of Si-O-Si, respectively [22,23]. A weak band was observed at 970 cm−1, which corresponded to the vibration of Si-OH groups formed as a by-product of incomplete condensation of the silanes. This result suggested that the silanes were completely hydrolyzed and polymerized to form a silica gel glass network during the sol–gel process. The FT-IR spectrum of the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass shared features with the SiO2 gel glass, implying that the introduction of CNDs had little influence on the IR spectrum of the SiO2 matrix because of the lowing doping level of the CNDs.

N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were used to provide details on the pore structures of the obtained gel glasses, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). All the samples exhibited a typical IV isotherm (definition by IUPAC), which is characteristic of mesoporous materials [24]. The appearance of H2 hysteresis loops at relative pressures indicated the presence of ink-bottle-type pores in these silica-based gel-glasses. Fig. 6 (b) shows the pore distributions calculated through Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) analysis of the desorption branch of the type IV isotherms [25].

In Fig. 5(b), the pore distributions of the gel glasses with different doping levels exhibited considerable differences. The silica gel glass matrix featured pores of a single size range, which was narrowly distributed at approximately 3.7 nm. These pores were attributed to the interspatial growth of silica-oxygen granules formed by hydrolysis and polycondensation of TEOS, GPTMS, and APPTES under acid catalysis through homogenous nucleation. After incorporation of the CNDs into the silica gel glass, the pore distributions changed markedly, all the CNDs/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses featured bimodal pore size distributions. For example, at a doping level of 5.5 × 10−4, the pore size distributions were centered at 3.90 and 5.08 nm. These results indicate that the presence of the CNDs changed the hydrolysis and polycondensation process of the metal alkoxides. The CNDs might provide effective inhomogeneous nucleation sites for silica-oxygen granules, inducing silica-oxygen granules to nucleate around them. As shown in the FT-IR spectra, the as-prepared CNDs possessed many groups, including amino, carbonyl, methylene, ether, and hydroxyl groups. These groups could participate in the polycondensation process and promote the sol-gel procedure, as indicated by the shorter sol-gel times. These two factors caused the TEOS, GPTMS, and APTES hydrolysis and polycondensation to be more complete than that in the absence of the CNDs; hence larger silica-oxygen granules and pores resulted. The pores concentrated at 3.90 nm were attributed to silica-oxygen granules formed by homogenous nucleation and the pores centered at 5.08 nm were attributed to silica-oxygen granules formed from inhomogeneous nucleation. The amount of pores corresponding to homogenous nucleation of silica-oxygen granules gradually decreased as those from the inhomogeneous nucleation of silica-oxygen granules increased. The pore size of the later also became larger as the doping level of the CNDs was increased.

The linear optical properties of the CNDs aqueous solution and CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass with different doping level were studied by UV–vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 6(a), aqueous solutions of the CNDs featured two absorption peaks at 215 and 262 nm. The former broad characteristic absorption peak was attributed to π−π* transitions of aromatic C=C bonds, and the latter band was attributed to n−π* transitions of C=O [26,27]. In the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses, the presence of the two adsorption bands further confirmed the successful introduction of CNDs into the silica gel glass. Note that the bands at 215 and 262 nm were red-shifted in gel glasses. The optical band gap of the CNDs was simply estimated from the UV–Vis absorption spectra with the following equation:
(1)
α = A [
( h ν −
E g
)
n / 2
/h ν ] ,
where α, ν, E
g , A, and n are the absorption coefficient, incident light frequency, band gap, a constant, and an integer, respectively. The band gap energy E
g values for the CNDs in aqueous solution and the CND/SiO2 gel glass with different doping level were determined from plots of (αhν)2 versus hν. Fig. 6 (b) shows the diagram used to calculate the band gaps. From the extrapolation of the straight line region of the Tauc plots in Fig. 6(b) (only the tangents of sample CND in aqueous solution and CND/SiO2 gel glass with doping level of 14.8 × 10−4 were presented in Fig. 6 (b) to keep the clarity of the figure), the values of E
g were determined to be 3.85, 3.62, 3.57, 3.50 and 3.44 eV for the aqueous solution of CNDs and the CND/SiO2 OMOSIL gel glass with doping level of 5.5, 7.4, 11.1, and 14.8 × 10−4, respectively. The red-shift and lower band gap of the CND/SiO2 gel glass can be explained by the participation of the CNDs in the hydrolysis and polycondensation of the sol-gel, which might influence delocalized states on the carbon surface, and the spatial structure of the CNDs and their steric hindrance. Furthermore, from Fig. 7 (c), it can be seen that the band gap of CND/SiO2 gel glass gradually became narrower with the increasing doping level. The narrowing band gap probably is due to the interaction between the CNDs become stronger with the increasing doping level and the overlap of the conduction band between CNDs.

Fig. 7 shows fluorescence spectra of the aqueous solution of CNDs, SiO2 gel glass, and the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the CNDs showed excitation dependent fluorescence similar to that previously reported [28,29]. When the excitation wavelength was between 300 and 340 nm the emission wavelength remained stable, indicating that a single fluorescent species dominated the emission of the dots in this UV region [30]. When the excitation wavelength was varied from 360 to 480 nm, the fluorescence spectra red-shifted and the intensity gradually decreased. This fluorescence behavior suggested the presence of different types of fluorophores having different degrees of double bond conjugation contributing to different emission wavelengths [31]. Conversely, the fluorescence spectrum of the SiO2 gel glass featured a broad emission band centered at approximately 410 nm. Similar, to the SiO2 gel glass, the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass also featured a wide band with additional peaks appearing at 450–475 nm, which could be attributed to CNDs. Furthermore, the incorporation of the CNDs considerably enhanced the luminous intensity of the gel glass matrix. However, unlike the aqueous solution of CNDs, the CND/SiO2 gel glass featured no obvious Stokes shift at different excitation wavelengths. This result is reasonable for the following reasons. Although excitation dependent photoluminescence behavior has been extensively reported for fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, the surface of the CNDs was changed modified on introduction into the silica matrix through the sol-gel procedure. This might cause the optical selection of different surface effects in the CNDs. Furthermore, in the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass, the micro-environment of the CNDs was completely different from that in aqueous solution. The pore structure results indicated there were two grades of pores existing in the CNDs/SiO2 gel glass, which would likely produce strong scattering, resulting in partial self-quenching of the CNDs.

OA Z-scan measurements were performed in the nanosecond regime at 532 nm to investigate the NLO and OL properties of CND aqueous solution and CND/SiO2 gel glasses. Fig. 8 (a) shows the OA results of the CND aqueous solution and CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass with the same linear transmittance of 80% at 532 nm. The laser input intensity was chosen to be approximately 15 μJ to agree with the thin sample assumption in Z-scan measurements. To exclude the contribution of SiO2 matrix, the Z-scan result of SiO2 matrix was also presented in Fig. 8(a). In the OA Z-scan, the transmittance of the sample is determined during its translation through the focal plane of a tightly focused beam. As the sample moves closer to the focus, the beam intensity increases and nonlinear effects occur, which results in decreased transmittance owing to NLA and/or NLS. The depth of the valley in the Z-scan curve directly determines the NLO performance. The CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass clearly showed considerably improved NLO performance compared with that of the CND aqueous solution. In addition, there is no obvious NLO response in SiO2 matrix, indicating that the observed NLO activity in the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass mainly resulted from doping CNDs. The enhanced NLO behavior of the CNDs in the solid silica gel glasses indicated good potential for solid-state OL devices.

To explore the advantage and potential application of CND/SiO2 gel glass in OL fields, similar measurement were also carried out on GO/SiO2 gel glass. Fig. 8(b) depicts the OA Z-scan curves for CND/SiO2 and GO/SiO2 gel glass measured at the same input fluencies with identical linear transmittance. The minimum transmittance at Z = 0 of CND/SiO2 gel glass comparable to GO/SiO2 gel glass. Therefore, the NLO performance of CND/SiO2 gel glass is comparable to that of the benchmark materials GO in gel glass.

This mechanism for the considerable enhancement of the NLO effect in the CND/SiO2 gel glass requires further investigation. Electrons from the valence band become excited to intermediate states, leading to the generation of a large number of free carriers when CNDs were irradiated. These free carriers can be further excited to higher levels within the conduction band by absorbing photons [32]. In particular, the fast recombination of free electrons with holes at non-radiative surface states can result in quenching effects, thus lowering the NLO response. Comparing to the CNDs in aqueous solution, the surrounding rigid matrix of the CNDs/SiO2 gel glasses can effectively decrease the probability of non-radiative relaxation and the presence of micropores enables carriers to be captured by defective states more easily when excited by high fluence laser. Thus, these effects reduce the carrier recombination rate and extending the excited state lifetime. This effect leads to an enhancement of the NLA of the CNDs and better NLO performance. However, it has been reported that inorganic semiconductors exhibit optical nonlinearities, which scale inversely with the optical energy gap [33]. Fig. 6(b) indicates that the band gap of the CNDs/SiO2 gel glass is lower than that in aqueous solution. The lower band gap leads to a reduction of the confinement energies of excitonic carriers and also contributes to the higher NLO response of the CND/SiO2 gel glass.

In addition, OA Z-scan results of the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses with different doping levels are shown in Fig. 9 (a). The value of the nonlinear extinction coefficient β can be fitted numerically to the following transmission equation for a third-order nonlinear process by the Crank–Nicolson finite-difference scheme [19]:
(2)
T
(

)

π

r 2
]
d r

Here, q
0
(z, 0) = βI
0
L
eff , where I0 is the on-axis peak intensity at the focus (z = 0), L
eff = [1 − exp(1−αl)]/α is the effective thickness of the sample, α is the linear absorption coefficient, and l is the sample thickness. The β values of gel glasses with different CNDs doping level are fitted by Eq. (1) and determined to be 0.84, 1.21, 1.68, and 4.15 cm/GW for CND doping level of 5.5, 7.4, 11.1, and 14.8 × 10−4. Meanwhile, the OL effects of the samples, manifested by plotting the normalized transmittance versus the input energy density, were calculated from the OA Z-scan measurements, as shown in Fig. 9(a). All of the samples present a similar trend, that is, the transmittance remains the same at low input fluence and then decreases as the fluence increases. This is a typical feature of OL materials. Meanwhile, the OA Z-scan data can be used to plot the variation of the sample transmission with the input laser fluence for a spatially Gaussian beam. The position-dependent light fluence
F
i n
( z )
at any position z can be calculated from the corresponding beam radius

)

.

The plots of the samples transmissions versus the input fluences are shown in Fig. 9(b). The OL is defined as the incident fluence at which the transmittance falls to 50% of the normalized linear transmittance was extracted to be > 5.5, 5.13, 4.51, and 2.68 J/cm2 for CND doping level of 5.5, 7.4, 11.1, and 14.8 × 10−4, respectively. Clearly, the values of β gradually increased while OL threshold decreased as the doping level of the CNDs increased, suggesting that a higher CND concentration leads to a better NLO response at 532 nm. This result suggests the NLO and OL properties of the composite gel glasses can be optimized by tuning the concentration of CNDs in the gel glass matrix.

In addition, the dependence of β in CND/SiO2 gel glasses on laser input energy is presented in Fig. 10 to reveal the input energy-relationship of the NLO behavior of the composites gel glasses. Obviously, for four doping levels, the β gradually increased with the input energy, implying CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glass showed enhanced NLO response when the excited input energy increase at all doping levels. This result confirmed that such materials can efficiently inhibit high energy laser and hence protect sensitive instruments and human eyes.

Generally, photon-induced thermal effect often occurred at nanosecond pulse irradiation owing to the wider laser pulse width as compared to picosecond or femtosecond pulse laser, which is also longer than the time needed to established thermal nonlinear effect. And therefore, we performed the OA Z-scan measurements of CND/SiO2 gel glass (with doping level of 11.1 × 10−4) at repetition frequencies of 1 Hz, 10 Hz and 20 H, respectively, to investigate the thermal effects in the composite gel glasses. As shown in Fig. 11 , the NLO responses of CND/SiO2 gel glass decreased with increasing repetition frequency, confirming the presence of photon-induced thermal effect in gel glasses at high repetition frequency. Under high repetition frequency irradiated, the thermal effect cannot effectively spread before the coming of next laser pulse next, and therefore result in a serious accumulation of thermal effects, making a sharp increase in the local temperature of the silica matrices. Consequently, the increased local temperature significantly influenced the NLO activity of CNDs in SiO2 matrices when excited by laser. However, applied lower repetition frequency can avoid the thermal effect. Meanwhile, how to effectively transfer the accumulated local thermal is the key factor to solve the thermal effect. We believe that improvement of thermal conductivity of the dopants and matrix as well as the interaction between them would be a solution, which is carrying out in our lab.

In summary, we have provided a simple and effective method for preparing highly transparent CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses. This simple route could be applied to the bulk preparation of other nanostructures. TEM, SEM, FT-IR spectroscopy, pore structure measurements, UV–vis spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectroscopy were performed to investigate the surface characteristic, structure, texture and linear optical properties of the CND/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses. Imaging studies and UV/Vis spectra confirmed the successful dispersion of CNDs in the ORMOSIL gel glass. The surface characteristics and pore structure of the host SiO2 matrix were markedly changed owing to the introduction of CNDs. The linear optical properties of the guest CNDs were changed during the sol-gel procedure. The NLO properties of the CNDs were investigated using a nanosecond OA Z-scan technique at 532 nm. We found that the NLO response of CNDs considerably improved after their incorporation into ORMOSIL gel glasses. In addition, the nonlinear extinction coefficient gradually increased as the doping level of the CNDs was increased, suggesting that the NLO properties of the composite gel glasses could be optimized by tuning the concentration of the CNDs in the gel glass matrix. Their processability renders these CNDs/SiO2 ORMOSIL gel glasses good candidates for next generation solid-state flexible optoelectronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Major Projects of the University of Fujian Province (Grant No. 2015N5007) and the Youth Natural Fund Key Project of Fujian Province (Grant No. JZ160462).
